feed.title: Combined summary - Weak Quantum Bounty Ceremony

entry.title: Combined summary - Weak Quantum Bounty Ceremony
entry.summary: The concept of a "quantum bounty" in Bitcoin involves creating a challenge that is directly tied to the ability to break a weaker cryptographic system, thereby testing the resilience and readiness of Bitcoin against potential quantum computational advances. The idea, as proposed by Nagaev Boris, is to generate a secp256k1 public key using a distributed ceremony where each participant contributes a secret share that remains undisclosed and uncombined at any point. This key, along with a ciphertext encrypted under a weaker elliptic curve system, forms a dual-layer cryptographic challenge. Both layers are constructed from the same hidden scalar, ensuring that recovery of the Bitcoin can only occur through the breach of the weaker system. This setup aims to serve as a publicly auditable target for testing quantum computational capabilities without requiring complex cryptographic solutions like SNARKs or trusted setups. Additionally, the discussion around this proposal highlights several perspectives on its relevance and utility. Critics, like Erik Aronesty, question the necessity of such bounties if quantum advancements could potentially threaten all funds secured by contemporary cryptography, suggesting that other motivations like academic recognition could drive researchers rather than direct financial incentives. Moreover, suggestions have been made to enhance the scheme’s privacy and deniability features to protect participants and contributors, potentially employing zero-knowledge proofs to obscure identities while maintaining robustness against censorship. Further technical suggestions include exploring different elliptic curves like secp160k1 and considerations for ensuring that private keys fall within specific ranges, to maintain integrity and security of the setup. Links to existing puzzles ([transaction details](https://mempool.space/tx/08389f34c98c606322740c0be6a7125d9860bb8d5cb182c02f98461e5fa6cd15)) illustrate the practical aspects of implementing such cryptographic challenges. These discussions reflect a broader debate about the balance between innovation in cryptographic practices and the realistic scenarios under which these innovations would be necessary or useful.
